questions.question: Which ISO/IEC standard provides AI concepts and terminology, but does not allow organisations to certify against it?

options.label: A
options.text: ISO/IEC 42005

options.label: B
options.text: ISO/IEC 42006

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 22989:2022 provides a comprehensive set of AI concepts and terminology to improve understanding and communication within the field of AI. Organisations cannot certify against it. ISO/IEC 42006 is the certification/auditing standard, making it a common distractor here.

questions.question: An organisation wants to conduct a formal assessment of the potential effects of its AI system on individuals and society, and document the findings. Which standard most directly supports this?

options.label: B
options.text: ISO/IEC 42005

options.label: C
options.text: ISO/IEC TR 24028

options.label: D
options.text: ISO/IEC 23053

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 42005 guides organisations in assessing the impact of AI systems on individuals and societies, and provides guidance on documenting those assessments. ISO/IEC 23894 is about risk management guidance generally, not specifically impact on people and society.

questions.question: Which standard is specifically the auditing and certification specification for ISO/IEC 42001?

options.label: A
options.text: ISO/IEC 42005

options.label: D
options.text: ISO/IEC 42006

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 42006 is the specification for auditing and certifying against ISO/IEC 42001. ISO/IEC 42005 is about impact assessments, and ISO/IEC 23894 covers AI risk management guidance — neither is the certification standard.

questions.question: ISO/IEC TR 24028 focuses primarily on which aspect of AI systems?

options.label: A
options.text: Terminology and concept definitions

options.label: B
options.text: Evaluating trustworthiness, including transparency and controllability

options.label: C
options.text: Risk management guidance for organisations using AI products

options.label: D
options.text: Frameworks for describing ML-based AI systems

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC TR 24028 focuses on evaluating the trustworthiness of AI systems, considering factors like transparency, explainability and controllability, and discusses mitigating vulnerabilities. ISO/IEC 23894 covers risk management (C), and ISO/IEC 23053 covers ML frameworks (D).

questions.question: Which of the following statements about ISO/IEC 23053 is correct?

options.label: A
options.text: It allows organisations to certify against it and is only relevant to private sector organisations

options.label: B
options.text: It introduces a framework for describing ML-based AI systems and is relevant to public, private and government organisations

options.label: C
options.text: It provides guidance on documenting AI system impact assessments

options.label: D
options.text: It is the standard used to audit and certify AI management systems

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 23053 introduces a framework for describing AI systems that use ML, with common terms and concepts, and is relevant to organisations of all types and sizes including public, private and government. Organisations cannot certify against it.

questions.question: The NIST AI Risk Management Framework core defines four functions. Which of the following correctly lists all four?

options.label: A
options.text: Plan, Do, Check, Act

options.label: B
options.text: Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond

options.label: C
options.text: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage

options.label: D
options.text: Govern, Assess, Treat, Monitor

questions.explanation: The NIST AI RMF Part 2 core defines four functions: Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage. Plan/Do/Check/Act is the PDCA cycle used in ISO standards, and Identify/Protect/Detect/Respond comes from the NIST Cybersecurity Framework — both common distractors.

questions.question: ISO/IEC 23894:2023 is described as relevant to organisations regardless of their size. What is its primary purpose?

options.label: A
options.text: Providing AI terminology for cross-organisational communication

options.label: B
options.text: Certifying organisations against AI risk management requirements

options.label: C
options.text: Providing guidance on AI risk management to enable and govern effective use of AI

options.label: D
options.text: Auditing AI management systems for compliance with ISO/IEC 42001

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 23894:2023 provides risk management guidance aimed at the highest management of an organisation to enable and govern effective use of AI. Organisations cannot certify against it, ruling out option B.

questions.question: ISO/IEC 42001 uses the word 'shall' throughout its requirements clauses. What does this signify?

options.label: A
options.text: The requirement is recommended but optional

options.label: B
options.text: The requirement is mandatory and must be met to conform to the standard

options.label: C
options.text: The requirement applies only to organisations with more than 50 employees

options.label: D
options.text: The requirement is guidance only, not subject to audit

questions.explanation: 'Shall' in ISO standards denotes a mandatory requirement. This is a deliberate convention used across ISO standards. Requirements expressed as 'shall' must be met for an organisation to conform and certify against the standard. 'Should' indicates a recommendation.

questions.question: ISO/IEC 42001 follows a harmonised structure. What is the primary benefit of this harmonised structure for organisations?

options.label: A
options.text: It makes the standard applicable only to technology companies

options.label: B
options.text: It ensures all annexes are normative rather than informative

options.label: C
options.text: It allows alignment and integration with other ISO management system standards

options.label: D
options.text: It removes the need for documented information requirements

questions.explanation: The harmonised (High Level Structure) allows ISO 42001 to align with other standards such as ISO 27001, ISO 27701 and ISO 9001, enabling integrated management systems. This is a deliberate design feature, not a limitation or simplification.

questions.question: According to ISO/IEC 42001, what is the definition of an AIMS?

options.label: A
options.text: A software platform for monitoring AI systems across an organisation's IT infrastructure

options.label: B
options.text: A set of interrelated or interacting elements intended to establish policies, objectives and processes to support responsible development, provision or use of AI systems

options.label: C
options.text: A risk register specifically focused on AI-related data breaches and system failures

options.label: D
options.text: A governance committee responsible for approving AI projects within an organisation

questions.explanation: Per ISO/IEC 42001, an AIMS is a set of interrelated or interacting elements of an organisation intended to establish policies and objectives as well as processes to support those objectives, in relation to responsible development, provision or use of AI systems. It is a management system, not a software tool or committee.

questions.question: In the AIMS structure, Clauses 4, 5, and 7 play a specific role. Which best describes that role?

options.label: A
options.text: They form the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle at the core of the AIMS

options.label: B
options.text: They are informative annexes providing supplementary guidance

options.label: C
options.text: They are supporting clauses that, if absent, jeopardise the operation of the core PDCA cycle

options.label: D
options.text: They govern only the documentation and audit requirements of the AIMS

questions.explanation: Clauses 4 (context), 5 (leadership) and 7 (support/resources) sit on the outside of the PDCA cycle and provide the foundation for it. Without these in place the core cycle of Plan (6), Do (8), Check (9), Act (10) cannot function effectively.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly maps the PDCA cycle to ISO/IEC 42001 clauses?

options.label: A
options.text: Plan=Cl 4, Do=Cl 5, Check=Cl 6, Act=Cl 7

options.label: B
options.text: Plan=Cl 6, Do=Cl 8, Check=Cl 9, Act=Cl 10

options.label: C
options.text: Plan=Cl 5, Do=Cl 6, Check=Cl 8, Act=Cl 9

options.label: D
options.text: Plan=Cl 7, Do=Cl 8, Check=Cl 9, Act=Cl 10

questions.explanation: The PDCA cycle in ISO/IEC 42001 maps as follows: Plan = Clause 6 (planning, objectives, risk), Do = Clause 8 (operation and controls), Check = Clause 9 (performance evaluation, audit, management review), Act = Clause 10 (improvement). Clauses 4, 5 and 7 support rather than form the cycle.

questions.question: Annex A of ISO/IEC 42001 is described as normative. What practical implication does this have for an organisation implementing an AIMS?

options.label: A
options.text: The organisation can read it for guidance but is not required to act on it

options.label: B
options.text: The organisation must apply every control listed in Annex A without exception

options.label: C
options.text: Controls selected from Annex A based on risk assessment become requirements; controls not selected must be justified

options.label: D
options.text: Annex A only applies to organisations that also hold ISO 27001 certification

questions.explanation: Because Annex A is normative, it is a fundamental part of the AIMS. The process is to review Annex A controls during clause 6 risk assessment, select applicable controls, and justify any exclusions via a Statement of Applicability. Organisations are not required to implement every control — selection is risk-based.

questions.question: What is the relationship between Annex A and Annex B in ISO/IEC 42001?

options.label: A
options.text: Annex A is informative and Annex B is normative — B overrides A

options.label: B
options.text: Annex A sets out control objectives and controls; Annex B provides implementation guidance for those same controls

options.label: C
options.text: Annex A covers risk sources and Annex B covers domain-specific guidance

options.label: D
options.text: Annex A and B are identical in content but targeted at different organisational sizes

questions.explanation: Annex A (normative) defines reference control objectives and controls. Annex B (also normative) provides implementation guidance for the controls listed in Annex A, with matching numbering. Annex B serves as a foundational reference but organisations may adapt implementations.

questions.question: Annexes C and D of ISO/IEC 42001 are described as informative. A compliance auditor tells an organisation they must implement all content from Annex C. Is this correct?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — informative annexes are still mandatory for certification purposes

options.label: B
options.text: No — informative annexes provide guidance and are not required; organisations must still identify applicable objectives and risks from their own context

options.label: C
options.text: Yes — Annex C is mandatory because it defines AI risk sources

options.label: D
options.text: No — informative annexes are only relevant to large enterprises

questions.explanation: Informative annexes provide guidance but are not mandatory requirements. Annex C lists potential AI-related objectives and risk sources, but it is explicitly not exhaustive and may not suit all organisations. The organisation must identify objectives and risks applicable to its own context.

questions.question: Under Clause 4.3 of ISO/IEC 42001, an organisation is defining the scope of its AIMS. What should the scope determination be based on?

options.label: A
options.text: The number of AI systems currently in production

options.label: B
options.text: The outputs of 4.1 (understanding the organisation and context) and 4.2 (needs and expectations of interested parties)

options.label: C
options.text: The requirements set out in Annex A only

options.label: D
options.text: Top management's preference for which business units to include

questions.explanation: Clause 4.3 requires the scope to be determined based on the work done in 4.1 (external and internal factors) and 4.2 (needs and expectations of interested parties). The scope defines which business processes and AI activities fall under the AIMS.

questions.question: A CEO delegates all AIMS responsibilities to the IT department and takes no further interest in it. Which clause of ISO/IEC 42001 does this most directly violate?

options.label: A
options.text: Clause 7.2 — Competence

options.label: B
options.text: Clause 6.1 — Actions to address risks and opportunities

options.label: C
options.text: Clause 5.1 — Leadership and commitment

options.label: D
options.text: Clause 9.3 — Management review

questions.explanation: Clause 5.1 requires top management (senior executives) to demonstrate leadership and commitment by aligning AI policy with strategic direction, ensuring resources, promoting effectiveness and fostering a responsible AI culture. Delegating entirely and disengaging fails this requirement directly.

questions.question: Under Clause 5.2, an organisation's AI policy must meet several requirements. Which of the following is NOT a requirement of the AI policy under ISO/IEC 42001?

options.label: A
options.text: Align with the organisation's purpose

options.label: B
options.text: Set the framework for AI objectives

options.label: C
options.text: Specify the exact controls to be implemented from Annex A

options.label: D
options.text: Be appropriately available and communicated to all interested parties

questions.explanation: Clause 5.2 requires the AI policy to align with organisational purpose, provide a framework for AI objectives, foster continual improvement, and be communicated to interested parties. Specifying exact Annex A controls is not a policy requirement — that happens through the risk assessment and treatment process in Clause 6/8.

questions.question: Under Clause 6.2, AI objectives must meet certain characteristics. Which of the following is NOT listed as a requirement for AI objectives?

options.label: A
options.text: Measurable

options.label: B
options.text: Aligned with policy

options.label: C
options.text: Approved by an external certification body

options.label: D
options.text: Available as documented information

questions.explanation: ISO/IEC 42001 Clause 6.2 requires AI objectives to be measurable, consider relevant requirements, be monitored, communicated, updated as necessary, and documented. External certification body approval is not a requirement for objectives — that is an audit and certification process, not a planning requirement.

questions.question: Clause 7.3 requires employees to be aware of three things in relation to the AIMS. Which answer correctly identifies all three?

options.label: A
options.text: The AI policy, their contribution to AIMS effectiveness, and consequences of non-compliance

options.label: B
options.text: The AI policy, all Annex A controls, and the organisation's risk register

options.label: C
options.text: Their role related to the AIMS, the scope of the AIMS, and documented evidence requirements

options.label: D
options.text: The AI policy, the organisation's strategic direction, and the management review schedule

questions.explanation: Clause 7.3 requires employees to be aware of: the AI policy, their role related to the AIMS, and the consequences of non-compliance. This is about ensuring staff understand their responsibilities and the importance of the AIMS, not detailed technical content.

questions.question: Clause 8.4 covers AI system impact assessment. How does this differ from AI risk assessment under Clause 8.2?

options.label: A
options.text: There is no meaningful difference — they are the same process with different names

options.label: B
options.text: Risk assessment looks at organisational risk; impact assessment focuses on the potential effects on people and society

options.label: C
options.text: Impact assessment applies only to AI systems that process personal data

options.label: D
options.text: Risk assessment is mandatory; impact assessment is optional guidance only

questions.explanation: Clause 8.2 (AI risk assessment) focuses on risks to the organisation's AIMS objectives. Clause 8.4 (AI system impact assessment) is explicitly broader — asking about the impact on people and society at large. Both are requirements, not optional.

questions.question: An organisation outsources part of its AI system development to a third party. Which clause most directly requires the organisation to address this?

options.label: A
options.text: Clause 5.3 — Roles, responsibilities and authorities

options.label: B
options.text: Clause 8.1 — Operational planning and control, which requires determining and controlling outsourced processes

options.label: C
options.text: Clause 9.2 — Internal audit

options.label: D
options.text: Clause 6.3 — Planning of changes

questions.explanation: Clause 8.1 explicitly requires organisations to determine and control outsourced processes. This is a direct requirement because outsourced AI development activities are still within the scope of the AIMS and the organisation remains responsible for conformance.

questions.question: When conducting an internal audit under Clause 9.2, which of the following requirements must be met regarding auditor selection?

options.label: A
options.text: Auditors must be external consultants to avoid bias

options.label: B
options.text: Auditors must be impartial — they should not audit their own work

options.label: C
options.text: The same auditor must conduct all internal audits for consistency

options.label: D
options.text: Auditors must hold a recognised ISO 42001 certification

questions.explanation: Clause 9.2 requires that auditors are impartial — they must not audit processes or areas they are responsible for. Auditors do not need to be external consultants; internal auditors can fulfil this role as long as impartiality is maintained. There is no requirement to hold a personal certification.

questions.question: Clause 9.3 requires management review. Who must perform this review and what should it assess?

options.label: A
options.text: The internal audit team must review compliance against Annex A controls quarterly

options.label: B
options.text: Top management must perform reviews at planned intervals to assess suitability, adequacy and effectiveness of the AIMS

options.label: C
options.text: External auditors must review the AIMS annually to maintain certification

options.label: D
options.text: The AI governance committee must review the risk register at each project milestone

questions.explanation: Clause 9.3 specifically requires top management (not audit teams or external bodies) to conduct management reviews at planned intervals, focusing on the suitability, adequacy and effectiveness of the AIMS. Results must be documented.

questions.question: Under Clause 10.2, a non-conformity is identified during an internal audit. What is the correct sequence of actions?

options.label: A
options.text: Document the non-conformity, then wait for the next management review to decide on action

options.label: B
options.text: Take appropriate actions, evaluate and implement those actions, review their effectiveness, make changes if needed, and keep documented information

options.label: C
options.text: Immediately suspend all AI operations until the non-conformity is resolved

options.label: D
options.text: Notify the certification body before taking any corrective action

questions.explanation: Clause 10.2 sets out a corrective action process: take appropriate actions, evaluate and implement them, review effectiveness, make further changes if needed, and maintain documented evidence. Waiting for management review or suspending operations is not the required approach.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly defines supervised machine learning?

options.label: A
options.text: ML that uses only unlabelled data to find patterns or structures

options.label: B
options.text: ML that makes use only of labelled data during training

options.label: C
options.text: ML that learns an optimal sequence of actions through interaction with an environment

options.label: D
options.text: ML that uses both labelled and unlabelled data during training

questions.explanation: Supervised ML uses only labelled data during training. Unsupervised ML uses only unlabelled data (A). Reinforcement learning learns through environmental interaction (C). Semi-supervised ML uses both labelled and unlabelled data (D).

questions.question: Deep learning is best described as:

options.label: A
options.text: A type of reinforcement learning that requires human feedback at each step

options.label: B
options.text: A symbolic AI approach that manipulates explicit rules and structures

options.label: C
options.text: A subset of ML that uses neural networks with many hidden layers to create rich hierarchical representations

options.label: D
options.text: An unsupervised ML technique that identifies clusters in unlabelled datasets

questions.explanation: Deep learning is a subset of ML that creates rich hierarchical representations through training neural networks with many hidden layers. It can reduce the need for feature engineering as relevant features are identified automatically. It is not a symbolic AI approach.

questions.question: The official ISO definition describes an AI system as 'an engineered system that generates outputs such as content, forecasts, recommendations or decisions for a given set of human-defined objectives.' Which four common features are also associated with AI systems?

options.label: A
options.text: Autonomous, Scalable, Connected, Reactive

options.label: B
options.text: Interactive, Contextual, Oversight, Adaptive

options.label: C
options.text: Transparent, Explainable, Auditable, Controllable

options.label: D
options.text: Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforced, Symbolic

questions.explanation: The four common features of AI systems identified in the course notes are: Interactive, Contextual, Oversight, and Adaptive. Options C reflects trustworthiness attributes from TR 24028, and D describes ML types.

questions.question: An organisation uses an AI model that was trained once and responds to user inputs based solely on that fixed training, with no ability to update or adapt. Which type of AI system does this best describe?

options.label: A
options.text: Limited memory AI

options.label: B
options.text: Theory of mind AI

options.label: C
options.text: General AI

options.label: D
options.text: Reactive machine AI

questions.explanation: Reactive machines respond to inputs based on fixed training without memory of past interactions or ability to adapt. Limited memory AI responds to instant events with a smaller pool of recent data (A). Theory of mind AI is a theoretical system capable of human-like emotions (B).

questions.question: Reinforcement learning is defined as:

options.label: A
options.text: Learning from labelled datasets where the correct output is provided for each input

options.label: B
options.text: An approach using neural networks with many hidden layers to refine outputs progressively

options.label: C
options.text: Learning an optimal sequence of actions to maximise reward through interaction with an environment

options.label: D
options.text: Learning that combines both labelled and unlabelled data to improve classification

questions.explanation: Reinforcement learning is defined as the learning of an optimal sequence of actions to maximise reward through interaction with an environment. It is distinct from supervised (A), semi-supervised (D) and deep learning (B).

questions.question: A financial services company deploys an AI credit scoring model. Customers cannot find out why they were rejected. Which AI principle is most directly violated?

options.label: A
options.text: Reliability and safety

options.label: B
options.text: Shared benefit

options.label: C
options.text: Transparency

options.label: D
options.text: Human control

questions.explanation: Transparency requires that stakeholders can understand how an AI system works, including its design decisions, objectives and safeguards. Being unable to explain a credit decision to a customer directly violates transparency. Fairness may also be relevant, but transparency is the most direct principle here.

questions.question: Which OECD AI principle is most directly concerned with the wider societal and economic impacts of AI, including whether it might increase inequality?

options.label: A
options.text: Transparency and explainability

options.label: B
options.text: Inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being

options.label: C
options.text: Accountability

options.label: D
options.text: Fostering a digital ecosystem for AI

questions.explanation: The OECD principle of inclusive growth, sustainable development and well-being explicitly considers whether AI might cause a greater divide in society and asks organisations to think about the wider impact of AI systems. Transparency and accountability are more process-focused.

questions.question: An AI hiring tool consistently ranks candidates from certain demographic backgrounds lower, even when qualifications are equivalent. Which AI principle is primarily violated?

options.label: A
options.text: Human control

options.label: B
options.text: Privacy and security

options.label: C
options.text: Fairness

options.label: D
options.text: Shared benefit

questions.explanation: Fairness requires that AI decisions do not produce discriminatory outcomes. This scenario — a hiring tool producing biased outcomes based on demographic background — is a direct example of fairness failure. CV reviewing is explicitly cited in the course notes as a fairness concern.

questions.question: In the context of the AI system lifecycle model, which of the following activities apply to ALL stages (including inception and retirement), rather than only to the middle development stages?

options.label: A
options.text: Design and development, verification and validation, deployment

options.label: B
options.text: Transparency and accountability, security and privacy, risk management, governance

options.label: C
options.text: DevOps processes and sprint-based iteration

options.label: D
options.text: Model training, feature engineering and hyperparameter tuning

questions.explanation: Transparency and accountability, security and privacy, risk management, and governance apply across all stages of the AI system lifecycle — from inception through to retirement. DevOps applies specifically to the middle stages (design, development, deployment, operation). Model training activities are a subset of development.

questions.question: An organisation wishes to integrate its AIMS with its existing ISO 27001 and ISO 9001 implementations. Which feature of ISO/IEC 42001 most directly supports this?

options.label: A
options.text: Annex D, which provides guidance on integrating AIMS with other management system standards

options.label: B
options.text: Clause 8.4, which requires AI system impact assessments

options.label: C
options.text: Annex C, which lists potential AI-related risk sources

options.label: D
options.text: Clause 10.2, which covers corrective actions for non-conformities

questions.explanation: Annex D (informative) specifically addresses the use of AIMS across domains and sectors, including guidance on integrating AIMS with other management system standards such as ISO 27001, ISO 27701 and ISO 9001. The harmonised structure of the standard also supports this, but Annex D is the most direct answer.
